Agent2 Round 1: The "fifth nerve" refers to the trigeminal nerve (cranial nerve V), which is responsible for sensation in the face and motor control of jaw movements including chewing. It specifically innervates the **mylohyoid** muscle (which elevates the hyoid bone during swallowing), the **anterior belly of the digastric**, and the **tensor tympani** muscle (involved in auditory reflexes). While some sources mention the posterior belly of the digastric being supplied by the hypoglossal nerve, there's no strong support here for the posterior belly receiving input from the trigeminal nerve. Additionally, although the stapes muscle is mentioned in one reference ("stapedius"), it is controlled via the facial nerve rather than the trigeminal. Therefore, only those components listed explicitly tied to the trigeminal nerve—such as mylohyoid, anterior belly of digastric, and tensor tympani—are correctly included among its branches.

[1] Neuroanatomy, Cranial Nerve 5 (Trigeminal) -- Muscles
The mandibular nerve is the only trigeminal nerve branch with a motor component. This branch supplies motor innervation to the mastication muscles, which include the masseter, temporalis muscle, and lateral and medial pterygoids. CN V3 also gives off branches that innervate the tensor veli palatini, mylohyoid, tensor tympani, and anterior digastric belly.
[2] Trigeminal Neuropathy -- Introduction -- Anatomy of CN V
Sensory supply includes most of the face from mid-cranium to the lower chin, including the eye and external ear, and the general sensation of anterior 2/3rds of the tongue. Motor branches innervate four muscles of mastication (temporalis, master, medial pterygoid, lateral pterygoid). They also supply 4 other muscles—mylohyoid, anterior belly of digastric, tensor tympani, and tensor veli palatini muscles. CN V also innervates parts of the sympathetic nervous system via V1 and the parasympathetic system via V1, V2, and V3, including the submandibular, sublingual, and parotid glands.
[3] Anatomy, Head and Neck, Digastric Muscle -- Nerves
The mylohyoid nerve innervates the anterior belly of the digastric. This nerve is a branch of the inferior alveolar nerve, which arises from the mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve. [6] The mandibular branch of the trigeminal nerve branches off before the inferior alveolar nerve penetrates the mandibular foramen. The inferior alveolar nerve then courses through the mylohyoid canal before giving off motor branches to both the mylohyoid muscle and the anterior digastric belly. [7] Branches of the facial nerve innervate the posterior belly of the digastric muscle.
[4] Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lateral Pterygoid Muscle -- Nerves
The lateral pterygoid muscle receives innervation from the mandibular branch of the trigenal nerve. The main trunk of the mandibular nerve divides into the anterior and posterior division. The anterior division gives off three motor branches. One of the motor branches is the nerve to the lateral pterygoid, a paired nerve (one for each belly) innervating the lateral pterygoid muscle. According to some authors, this muscle may also receive innervation from the anterior, middle, and posterior deep temporal nerves, the masseteric nerve, and sometimes from the buccal nerve.
